stories.headline: Avalanche rally from 3-0 deficit to beat Wild in OT, advance to Western Conference Final
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche overcame a three-goal deficit to defeat the Minnesota Wild 4-3 in overtime in Game 5 of their second-round playoff series Wednesday at Ball Arena, advancing to the Western Conference Final. Brett Kulak scored the series-clinching goal at 3:52 of overtime — the first of his NHL career and the 10th overtime series-clincher in Avalanche franchise history.

Nathan MacKinnon scored the game-tying goal for Colorado, while Parker Kelly and Jack Drury also tallied. Brent Burns and Martin Necas each recorded two assists, and Scott Wedgewood stopped all seven shots he faced in relief after entering the game mid-contest.

The comeback ranks among the most dramatic in recent Avalanche playoff history, with the team erasing what had been a commanding Wild lead. Colorado will now face the winner of the other Western Conference second-round series in the Conference Final.

GM Chris MacFarland, a finalist for General Manager of the Year, saw his résumé bolstered considerably by the victory. Brett Kulak's 16th different goal scorer in the series underscored the team's offensive depth throughout the playoff run.

stories.headline: Colorado General Assembly adjourns 120-day legislative session; key leaders depart
stories.summary:
The Colorado Legislature concluded its 2026 regular session Wednesday evening, with both chambers finishing their business around 7:15 p.m. after 120 days of lawmaking conducted under the constraints of a billion-dollar budget shortfall. The session marked the final day in office for several top legislative leaders, including House Speaker Julie McCluskie, House Majority Leader Monica Duran and Senate Majority Leader Robert Rodriguez, all of whom are term-limited or seeking other offices.

Among the bills approved in the final hours were a requirement to release body-worn camera footage to family members of people killed by police, expanded protections for students with disabilities, boosted background checks for ride-hailing drivers, and a requirement that abortion medication be available on college campuses. Lawmakers also created a new security advisor role at the Capitol.

The Senate passed amended legislation designed to neutralize a November ballot measure, Initiative 175, that would direct more money to road funding. The amendment creates a new enterprise fund for road maintenance funded by fees from commercial trucks and manufactured home hauling permits. A bill that would have created a state-level general duty clause for workplace safety was voted down, with six Democrats joining Republicans in opposition.

McCluskie, the first female Western Slope speaker, closed her eight-year House tenure with a call for bipartisan problem-solving. The legislature killed a bill requiring in-person purchases of firearm barrels and left several major fiscal reform efforts unfinished heading into the interim period.

stories.headline: Gov. Polis veto threat kills Democratic bills to roll back business tax breaks and fund family child tax credit
stories.summary:
Two Democratic bills that would have rolled back business tax deductions to fund an expanded child tax credit for lower-income Colorado families were killed Monday in the Senate Finance Committee after Gov. Jared Polis threatened to veto them. House Bills 1221 and 1222 had already cleared the House before sponsors pulled them at the committee stage.

House Bill 1221 would have reduced the deductibility of CEO pay and shortened the business loss carryforward period, generating an estimated $124 million annually. House Bill 1222 would have eliminated state tax write-offs for business equipment, machinery, and research and development costs, expected to raise up to $329 million per year. Together the measures would have enabled a refundable child tax credit of up to roughly $1,000 per child for families earning up to $95,000.

Polis's office said the governor supports income tax cuts as a condition of any business tax rollback, a position at odds with the bills' Democratic sponsors. Senate sponsor Cathy Kipp said the governor wanted a state income tax reduction to accompany any changes. Many of the targeted business deductions were created or expanded under federal legislation signed by President Trump.

House sponsors reacted sharply to the outcome. Rep. Lorena Garcia accused the governor of overriding a co-equal branch of government, while Rep. Yara Zokaie said she was "extremely disappointed" the Senate failed to act on child poverty. The defeat leaves intact a yearslong Democratic effort to reshape Colorado's tax code that has yet to produce major changes.

stories.headline: Dozen indicted in Front Range card skimming ring that targeted EBT cardholders at 7-Eleven stores
stories.summary:
The Boulder County District Attorney's Office announced Tuesday that 12 people — 11 adults and one juvenile — have been indicted for operating a major card skimming crime ring that stole financial information from hundreds of victims across the Front Range. Eight of the 12 have been arrested, including Romario Ciuciu, Viorel Tudor, Ion Ciuciu, Felicia Ciuciu, Denisa Barbu, Ana Maria Dumitru, Madalina Velcu and the juvenile.

The group is accused of installing skimming devices on ATMs and point-of-sale machines at convenience stores — particularly 7-Eleven locations — that covertly recorded card numbers and PINs. The stolen data was used to create cloned cards, which were then used to drain victims' accounts. The ring particularly targeted Colorado EBT cardholders, stealing or attempting to steal at least $301,400 from at least 447 EBT card accounts over less than six months.

Law enforcement seized 236 cloned cards during the investigation, each containing a victim's financial data. The charges include violations of the Colorado Organized Crime Control Act, cybercrime, identity theft, possession of forgery devices, criminal possession of financial devices and evidence tampering. The investigation was led by the Lafayette Police Department with assistance from the Colorado Bureau of Investigation.

Boulder DA Michael Dougherty credited Lafayette police for connecting seemingly unrelated offenses to dismantle the operation. Investigators advise consumers to look for scratches near card terminals and PIN pads as signs of tampering, and recommend using tap-to-pay options when available.

stories.headline: Person of interest held after second fire in two days hits Castle Rock restaurant
stories.summary:
Castle Rock police took a person of interest into custody Wednesday after a second fire struck Hideaway Bar & Grill at 600 Jerry Street within 48 hours, though the individual was arrested on unrelated charges. Firefighters arrived around 12:30 a.m. Wednesday to find flames and smoke coming from the building and quickly contained the blaze with no injuries reported.

The first fire, which struck the restaurant Monday night, caused extensive damage to the kitchen and put 21 employees out of work. Wednesday's fire affected a different part of the building. Co-owner Robert Stroh, who has been in the restaurant business for 40 years, said he was alerted to Monday's fire by his alarm company and described the back-to-back incidents as "unbelievable."

The cause of both fires remains under investigation, and it has not been confirmed whether the person of interest is connected to the first blaze. Authorities have not publicly identified the individual in custody.

Co-owner Becky Stroh launched a GoFundMe campaign to assist the 21 displaced employees. Robert Stroh said the fundraising goal is to help workers bridge the financial gap while they find new jobs, not to benefit the owners themselves.

stories.headline: Teen in custody after driving stolen car through Aurora high school parking lot
stories.summary:
A 17-year-old was taken into custody Wednesday after allegedly driving a stolen vehicle through the parking lot of Overland High School in Aurora, according to Aurora police. The teen had not been apprehended at the time of Tuesday's incident but was detained following a separate incident on Wednesday. No shots were fired and no injuries were reported in either event.

Aurora police did not release additional details about the nature of Wednesday's separate incident that led to the arrest. The case involves a juvenile suspect and is being handled accordingly by law enforcement.

The incident drew attention from students, parents and school officials at Overland High School. Aurora Police Department confirmed the student was detained and the situation was resolved without violence.

stories.headline: Denver could hit first 90-degree day of 2026 midweek as summer-like heat returns to Front Range
stories.summary:
A ridge of high pressure is pushing temperatures across the Denver metro area toward 90 degrees Wednesday, which would mark the city's first 90-degree day of 2026 and potentially tie a record high last set in 1915. Forecasters say isolated gusty afternoon showers and storms are also possible Wednesday along the I-25 corridor, in the mountains and on the Eastern Plains, though no severe weather is expected.

The warming trend is forecast to continue through the week, with Denver potentially approaching another record high Thursday, when temperatures could reach the mid-80s. The current Thursday record is 87 degrees, set in 2013. Weekend temperatures are expected to remain in the low 80s, with isolated storms possible Sunday afternoon.

A more significant cool-down with rain is expected to return to the region by Monday and Tuesday of next week, bringing temperatures back toward seasonal norms. Residents are urged to take heat precautions, particularly for children, elderly individuals and outdoor workers during peak afternoon hours.

stories.headline: Apartment fire injures one at Willows at Tamarac complex in south Denver, closes Hampden Avenue
stories.summary:
A fire broke out Wednesday afternoon at The Willows at Tamarac apartment complex in south Denver, sending flames and smoke through the building's eaves and injuring at least one person. Denver Fire Department crews arrived within minutes of the initial report and worked to contain the blaze while an emergency evacuation order was issued for residents just after 2:45 p.m.

The injured person was transported to a hospital, though their condition was not immediately disclosed. Denver Police closed East Hampden Avenue in both directions between Yosemite and Verbena streets to accommodate the fire response.

The cause of the fire was not immediately determined. The extent of damage to the multi-unit residential building was not available in initial reports, and it was unclear how many residents were displaced by the evacuation order.

stories.headline: Douglas County shelter-in-place lifted after vehicle crashes into building, breaks gas line near Parker
stories.summary:
A shelter-in-place order affecting several Douglas County neighborhoods was issued and lifted Wednesday afternoon after a vehicle crashed into a building near South Chambers Road and East Mainstreet in the Parker area, rupturing a gas line. South Metro Fire Rescue responded at 1:14 p.m. and cleared the order by 2:45 p.m., though officials noted a lingering gas smell could remain in the area.

The original order covered a half-mile radius from 10759 Tomboy Drive, including the Newlin Crossing, Sierra Ridge and Meridian Village neighborhoods. Pine Grove Elementary School was placed under shelter-in-place and conducted a controlled release of students. Chambers Road was closed for several hours between Mainstreet and Cosmopolitan Circle.

Paramedics transported two people to area hospitals for evaluation, though it was unclear whether they were occupants of the vehicle or nearby residents. Photos released by South Metro officials showed a red pickup truck had left the roadway on northbound Chambers and crashed into a shed. The cause of the crash was not immediately available.

stories.headline: CDOT's Colorado Boulevard Bus Rapid Transit plan faces opposition from City of Glendale
stories.summary:
The Colorado Department of Transportation held its second public open house Wednesday on plans to bring Bus Rapid Transit service to Colorado Boulevard, a seven-mile corridor running from the 40th and Colorado RTD Station south to the Southmoor Station. The project would add dedicated bus lanes to a road CDOT has identified as part of the region's High Injury Network, with the agency projecting daily ridership could double from the current roughly 3,000 passengers.

The plan faces a significant obstacle in Glendale, the small city whose approximately one mile of the corridor runs between Mississippi and Alameda avenues. Glendale's city council voted unanimously last week to formally oppose all proposed alternatives and recommend a "no build" outcome. Deputy City Manager Josh Bertrand cited concerns about construction impacts to local businesses and pointed to the ongoing East Colfax BRT project as a cautionary example of disruption.

Glendale also cited declining vehicle counts at every measured intersection on the corridor over the past two decades, arguing the data undercuts CDOT's growth projections. Three design options remain under consideration: a mixed-flow configuration, a side-running dedicated lane option, and a center-plus-side-running option that CDOT's own data shows could more than double southbound commute times for drivers traveling the full corridor.

CDOT is currently in the Alternatives Analysis phase and has not selected a preferred design. Supporters, including the Denver Streets Partnership, gathered signatures outside Wednesday's open house in favor of the BRT project, arguing dedicated transit infrastructure is needed to improve safety and mobility along one of the metro area's most dangerous roads.

stories.headline: United Airlines moves closer to construction on 113-acre development near Denver International Airport
stories.summary:
United Airlines' planned 113-acre development near Denver International Airport, which has been in the works since 2023, is moving closer to the construction phase after clearing a key regulatory hurdle, according to the Denver Business Journal. Details of the specific approval and the project's timeline were advancing as of Wednesday.

The development is expected to be a significant commercial project in the northeast Denver metro area near DIA, one of the region's major economic hubs. United Airlines has been among the airport's anchor tenants and largest employers in the area for decades.

Further details on the scope of the project, expected jobs, and construction timeline were not fully disclosed in initial reporting. The project represents one of the larger private development efforts planned in the DIA corridor.

stories.headline: Rockies sit at 16-25 at season's quarter mark, on pace for 20-game improvement over 2025
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies have reached the quarter mark of the 2026 MLB season with a 16-25 record, a pace that would represent a roughly 20-game improvement over their 2025 finish. While still well below .500, the trajectory has drawn attention given how far the franchise fell last season.

The Rockies continued their road schedule Wednesday, facing the Pittsburgh Pirates at PNC Park. Infielder Edouard Julien has been a contributor this season, posting a .260 batting average, .358 on-base percentage and .724 OPS through 120 plate appearances, with two home runs and 12 RBI.

Whether the early-season improvement reflects genuine roster development or scheduling factors remains a question for the organization. The Rockies have been in an extended rebuilding phase and any measurable progress in the win column would be viewed as a positive sign by the front office and fan base.

weather.notable: About 16 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: My wife asked me to fix the dryer. I looked at it. I looked at her. She called the repair guy. That's teamwork.

topStory.headline: South Carolina Governor Expected to Call Special Session to Redraw Congressional Maps in Push for Republican Sweep
topStory.summary:
South Carolina Gov. Henry McMaster is expected to announce a special session as early as Thursday to address a stalled redistricting effort after state lawmakers failed to reach consensus before the end of the regular legislative session. At issue is a proposed congressional map backed by House Republicans that supporters claim could position the party to win all seven of South Carolina's U.S. House seats, up from its current six.

The map was developed with input from Adam Kincaid, executive director of the National Republican Redistricting Trust, who described it as a 'very solid potential' plan for a 7-0 Republican advantage. However, five Republican senators joined Democrats in blocking a resolution to advance the redistricting work into a special session, exposing intraparty divisions. Senate Majority Leader Shane Massey, a Republican from Edgefield, warned that an overly aggressive map could backfire, saying the outcome could be 6-1 or even 5-2 if lawmakers overreach. House Republican Rep. Luke Rankin of Laurens pushed back, arguing the map clearly supports a 7-0 result and accusing the Senate of failing to engage seriously in the process.

The redistricting push follows a U.S. Supreme Court ruling that struck down provisions of the Voting Rights Act protecting against racial discrimination in voting and representation, prompting Republican-led states across the country to pursue more aggressively partisan congressional maps. A referendum in Virginia that produced additional Democratic-leaning seats also accelerated Republican efforts to redraw boundaries in states where they hold legislative power, though Virginia's state supreme court subsequently struck down those results. President Donald Trump has been directly involved in the pressure campaign, personally calling state lawmakers to encourage action on redistricting.

The full South Carolina House has not yet voted on maps advanced by a committee earlier this week, and details about how a special session would proceed remain unclear. McMaster declined to take questions at a Statehouse appearance Thursday but his office indicated an announcement was likely that afternoon. The outcome carries significant implications for the balance of power in the U.S. House of Representatives, where control is expected to be decided by a narrow margin following this year's midterm elections.
